name: Community event planning and logistics
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The user is preparing for Mars College 2026, a 3-month off-grid desert program (January-March) focused on Creative AI, where they'll participate in Gene's AI Camp track. They have 25 years of metal fabrication and themed entertainment experience, plus former experience as a medic. During a recent 30-person logistics meeting with organizers Jon Miller, Gene, Vincent, and Freeman, the user volunteered to set up a medical aid station and help with build phase construction work. They're facing a financial challenge where they need to work in December for income but want to contribute to the early build phase that starts in December.

The user has been developing a sophisticated AI project called the "Benjamin Franklin Engine" (also referred to as Cognitive Feedback Engine), which evolved from a simple dual-LLM concept into a four-layer hierarchical cognitive architecture. The system includes a Captain layer for strategic planning, an Orchestrator for tactical execution, Creative and Analytical modes for cognitive processing, a Virtue Engine for persistent memory, and an economic governance system using token budgets. They sent Gene their presentation materials and briefing documents about this project an hour before the logistics meeting, positioning it as potential curriculum content for the AI Camp program.

The user is strategically positioning themselves to find career opportunities, particularly with Eden.art (which provides compute resources for Mars participants and is affiliated with Gene). They want to contribute meaningfully to the Mars College community while also leveraging their technical work and skills for potential professional advancement. Their approach demonstrates confidence in their value proposition, having shown initiative during the meeting by volunteering first for introductions and offering their medical expertise when appropriate. They plan to visit Bombay Beach in early December for face-to-face discussions and are taking a measured "play it by ear" approach to see how Gene responds to their cognitive architecture project while maintaining focus on their confirmed contributions of medical station setup and build phase support.

CONTEXTUAL:

attachments.extracted_content:
For the past few years, we’ve organized a creative AI study at Mars College. It has included classes, workshops, and group activities aimed at helping people learn how to use AI tools for their own art, research, and personal projects. This year marks the fourth year of the program and we’re more excited about it than ever.

Apply to Mars College

Wait, what is Mars College? Read here.

Martian Academics
Mars College isn’t a webinar or virtual class. It’s a solar-powered off-grid campus built from the ground up each winter in a dry lakebed in the California desert. Martians assemble their own living spaces, kitchens and labs, then roll a curriculum that merges hands‑on homesteading with self‑guided research. At the end of the semester, campus is disassembled and disbanded, to reconvene at the same time next year. This rugged lifestyle isn’t for everyone; it’s harsh, dusty, and tests your ingenuity and resilience. But it forges a tight-knit community of learners and refuge from the hamster wheel of urban professional life.




Digital architecture workshop by Andrew Heom, 2023
Mars College extends the self‑reliance ethos of desert communities to the technology domain; rather than fall for the nature vs technology dichotomy, we think AI is the secret superpower and enabler of the 21st century vagabond, enabling intrepid individuals to acquire new skills and knowledge quickly and cheaply, from even the most remote locations on Earth, no internet link required!

Academics at Mars are a mixture of a structured curriculum that aims to level up everyone’s literacy around contemporary AI tools, plus an unconference-style open calendar where participants propose and host their own sessions on diverse topics ranging from permaculture to creative writing to singing sea shanties.

AI at Mars College 2022-2025
The Creative AI major at Mars College was first formed in 2022 and focused on early image generation and chatbots.




Early AI experiments at Mars, via X.
As generative video and agents evolved, so too did our program. In the wake of the AI explosion of the last few years, the program grew in attendance and scope. Last year we began a serious study of AI agents, highlighted by several dozen experimental chatbots we placed in our private Discord server, each with a Martian creator learning on the fly how to make their agent represent their values.

Mars Research Curriculum
Mars College
·
October 20, 2024
Mars Research Curriculum
Mars College is gearing up for a new season, and one of the new camps this year is called Mars Research. Mars Research is dedicated to the study of AI, and this upcoming academic program will focus on generative AI. Let’s explore the tracks and courses that will shape the program.

Read full story
In early 2023, leading up to the Bombay Beach Biennale, despite the then-lack of commercially available AI video models, we collectively produced our first ever AI film festival. As AI video models rapidly progressed, the AI film academy formed and made the film festival a tradition. We are planning the 2026 film festival and will be guiding aspiring Martian creators learn how to produce the kinds of films that just a few years ago would have cost tens of millions of dollars to produce.


Past editions of AI program at Mars College

The 2026 program
Announcing Mars College 2026
Mars College
·
Aug 9
Announcing Mars College 2026
Mars College is coming back for another semester in the desert, and we’d love for you to join us.

Read full story
Now going into its 5th edition, the creative AI program is expanding into new frontiers, including a planned game development track. Even as we delve into abstract and heady topics like world models, the purpose remains deeply practical. By studying how to build and manipulate such AIs, participants learn how to assemble personal systems that assist them in everything from learning home‑grown solar arrays to making money online to support their itinerant lifestyle.

Our aim is to help every participant build a personal AI: a bespoke agent that augments their learning, helps with creative projects and makes life on Mars more interesting. We give students free compute on Eden.art and teach them how to wield these models.

Over the coming weeks, we plan to announce more about the AI program. If you’re already psyched about the program, apply today! Otherwise, stay tuned for more details by subscribing here.

Mars College
Mars College


Announcing Mars College 2026
An open call to participate in our high-tech, off-grid, pop-up college
Mars College
Aug 09, 2025




Mars College is coming back for another semester in the desert, and we’d love for you to join us.

Apply to Mars College 2026

Mars College is an annual 3-month program dedicated to helping people adopt a low-cost, high-tech, self-sufficient life. We are equal parts lifestyle incubator, educational program, and intentional community. Mars College is located next to the gorgeous Salton Sea in Southern California.

In the six years we’ve been doing this, many Martian alumni have called it the best experience of their lives.

Here’s how it works:

Phase 1: Build (Nov-Dec 2025)
Before most Martians arrive, a small crew builds an off-grid village in the open desert a mile outside the town of Bombay Beach, and equips it with water, solar power, and high-speed internet.






Builds from years past
Phase 2: Learn (Jan-Mar 2026)
Most Martians arrive on or around New Years. The first two weeks are dedicated to post-build decoration and outfitting our campus for life. The second week is also Ideas Week, a week of orientation, presentations, and getting to know each other.




Lightning talks and dinners during Ideas Week
The first half of the semester is an unconference-style academic program. There is an open calendar for Martians to offer workshops sharing their skills and passions. There are a number of academic tracks and topics including (but not limited to): Creative AI, bodywork, vibecoding, off-grid building, permaculture, creative writing, game development, music-making, science fiction and more. Every day is filled with classes, workshops and events created by Martians.










Living and learning in the desert
The second half of the semester is more loose in structure, and classes give way to collaborations and group project work in preparation for our semester-end final exhibition, Mars Electronica.









Mars Electronica includes performances, a gallery, a film festival, and final presentations from Martians
Phase 3: Unbuild (Apr 2026)
Mars College is dismantled in mid-April, and we leave the land as barren as it was when we got there, to wait until next year to do it all over again.




Left without a trace
Over the next few weeks, we will be sharing more info about our upcoming plans, on our Substack, Instagram, and Twitter.

FAQ
Why are you doing this?
Why not? Mars College is an experiment in what’s possible. We appear, build our campus, get to know one another, make friends, learn new things, make art, showcase our work, and disappear. Then we do it all again.

Where will I live? What’s it like to live there?
Living on Mars is a choose-your-own-adventure situation. Generally, Martians join a living co-op to meet their basic needs such as food and shelter. If you are a new Martian, this will be part of the interview process.

Martians living on Mars can build dorms or other shelter, camp, or live in a vehicle. The Mars campus will have several kitchens stewarded by the living co-ops, and water, internet, and solar power are provided as campus services.

Some Martians elect to get housing in Bombay Beach, which is also possible, for those who prefer a house. You can make your preferences clear in the application.

How much does it cost?
This will depend on how you choose to structure your living situation while on Mars. For the three-month program, expect Mars College dues to be somewhere around $500-1,000 total. This is used to cover the cost of high-speed internet, portapotties, water, and building materials. This notably does not include your food/grocery costs, which are generally arranged separately.

Sounds great, how do I apply?
Fill out the application form here. We will start having interviews and rolling admissions in late August/early September.

Apply to Mars College 2026

chat_messages.text:
**Meeting transcript key points:**

**Building layout (Freeman's design):**
- Main building = Chiba (flat roof with deck access this year)
- AI Camp = attached second building (slanted roof)
- Three deep bays (40+ feet each) underneath
- Kitchen in one corner with corner windows (north/west facing)
- Mimos space (partially covered, shade net)
- Gallery spaces on ground floor
- Classroom upstairs (one bay)
- Workshop/desk space upstairs (other bay)
- Raised floors (pallet style, not dust)
- Projection wall setup for outdoor cinema/events
- Protected quad layout for wind resistance

**Jon's callouts:**
- Spreadsheet for arrivals coming soon (Discord)
- People building = showing up early December
- Mark when you're arriving to coordinate

**Gene's AI Camp curriculum:**
- First half: LLM fundamentals, agents, tokens, multi-agent systems
- Second half: Film festival + Mars Electronica production
- Open calendar for workshops/contributions
- "Gigabrain" - community coordination agent that knows everyone
- Real-time voice interfaces throughout Chiba
- "Living building" concept - sensors, terminals, AI integration everywhere
- Building on last year's SolarSAM (Dracula AI for solar/building systems)

**Your position so far:**
- Medical station setup (former medic)
- Available for build phase with Freeman
- Fabrication/themed entertainment background

**New opportunity mentioned:**
- **Nightly cinema production** - someone wants to make it "ceremonial event production, making cool spaces homie"
- This ties directly into your projection surface setup, themed design, LED work

**What's clicking:**
You could own the **cinema experience design** - not just projection tech, but the whole vibe. Cozy seating, lighting design, ceremonial atmosphere. That's literally your wheelhouse (themed entertainment + LED systems).

Want to flag that as a potential lane? Or staying focused on medical + build support?
can i get a full report this meeting?

# AI Camp Meeting Report - Nov 23, 2025

## Attendees
**Organizer:** Jon Miller (jon@jmill.dev)  
**Key Figures:** Gene (kogan.gene@gmail.com), Vincent (vincent@drmbt.com), Freeman, Chebel, and ~27 other participants

---

## BUILDING & INFRASTRUCTURE (Freeman presenting)

### Main Structure Overview
- **Chiba** (main building) - flat roof with deck access (change from previous slanted design)
- **AI Camp building** (attached second structure) - slanted roof for easier waterproofing
- **Three deep bays** underneath - 40+ feet deep each
- Protected quad layout designed for wind resistance
- Raised floors throughout (pallet-style, not dust)

### Space Allocations

**Ground Floor:**
- **Kitchen** - Fully enclosed, corner windows (north/west facing, 8ft windows), similar to Freeside's cozy window setup
- **Gallery spaces** - Left and right wings of Chiba
- **Mimos** - Third bay on right side, partially covered with shade net over solid floor
- **Open middle section** - Couch areas, space under stairs (similar to Tsar location last year)

**Upper Level:**
- **Classroom** - One bay, deep space with screen in back (similar to last year)
- **Workshop/desk space** - Second bay, possibly desks along north wall like Tool Camp had
- Two-thirds solid roof coverage, one-third shade net only

**Outdoor Features:**
- Large steps on northwest corner of Chiba facing Bombay Beach (changed from north-facing)
- Dedicated projection wall setup with protected enclosure for permanent outdoor cinema
- Potential for cozy movie event space in protected quad area

### Behind the Scenes
- Solar installation lives behind screen with additional storage space available
- No library space allocated in Chiba/AI Camp (possibly moving to Freeside)

---

## INFRASTRUCTURE NEEDS (Still being determined)

### Coordination Required:
- **Water coordination** - System TBD
- **Pantry setup/management** - Getting organized
- **Power distribution** - Kitchen electrical needs
- **Flooring installation** - Build phase work
- **Kitchen Lead role** - Open position needing coordinator

### Environment & Logistics
- **Power:** Available most of the time, laptops/computers fine
- **Internet:** Pretty good connectivity
- **Weather:** Cold at night, especially January - "feels like freezing" even if not technically freezing
- **Dust:** Usually minimal, but wind can kick it up (mask/goggles situational)
- **Food:** One restaurant nearby, no real store. Meal plan details TBD at beginning, need shopping/fridge coordination system
- **Medical:** Hospital is far away - need on-site medical station planning

---

## AI CURRICULUM & PROGRAM (Gene presenting)

### First Half of Semester (Jan-Feb)
**LLM Fundamentals Course:**
- Introduction to large language models
- Understanding agents - how they actually work
- Tokens, assistant messages, architecture
- Multi-agent systems and interactions
- Small-scale experiments and hands-on learning

### Second Half of Semester (Feb-Mar)
**Production Focus:**
- Film festival preparation
- Mars Electronica final exhibition work
- Structured curriculum to be detailed closer to dates

### Special Projects

**Gigabrain:**
- Community coordination agent
- Knows all Martians
- Helps coordinate community activities
- Multi-agent simulations

**Living Building Concept:**
- Integration of all AI systems throughout Chiba
- Real-time voice interfaces in every room
- Sensor data from buildings (electricity usage, etc.)
- Multiple electronic terminals throughout campus
- Evolution of last year's SolarSAM (Dracula-voiced AI for solar/building systems)
- Goal: Fully interactive, AI-aware living space

**Tools & Resources:**
- Eden.art compute access for all participants
- Advanced image generation tools (demonstrated live during call)
- Voice interface systems
- Collaborative human-AI projects

### Open Calendar System
- Unconference-style workshop scheduling
- Participants can propose and host sessions
- AI Camp contributing to broader Mars curriculum
- Framework being developed for submissions

---

## PRODUCTION OPPORTUNITIES

### Nightly Cinema
- Interest in making this a "ceremonial event production"
- Focus on creating "cool spaces, homie"
- Permanent projection surface planned
- Cozy gathering space design needed

---

## ACTION ITEMS & TIMELINE

### Immediate (Now - Early December)
1. **Check AI Camp Discord regularly** - Primary coordination channel
2. **Watch for arrival spreadsheet** - Mark your arrival date when posted
3. **Coordinate early December arrival if participating in build phase**

### Build Phase (Early-Mid December)
- Small crew arrives to construct infrastructure
- Freeman needs help with various building tasks
- Kitchen setup and power coordination
- Medical station planning

### Main Arrival (Around New Year's)
- Most Martians arrive
- Ideas Week (Week 2) - Orientation, presentations, getting to know community

### Semester Structure (Jan-Mar)
- First half: Structured AI curriculum + open workshop calendar
- Second half: Looser structure, collaborative projects, Mars Electronica prep
- Final exhibition: Mid-April before unbuild

---

## OPEN ROLES & OPPORTUNITIES

### Confirmed Needs:
- **Kitchen Lead** - Coordination for kitchen infrastructure, pantry, shopping, meal planning
- **Medical Station Setup** - Planning and implementation (you volunteered)
- **Build Phase Support** - Various tasks with Freeman (you volunteered)
- **Nightly Cinema Production** - Event design and execution

### Your Potential Contributions:
- Medical station/first aid planning (former medic experience)
- Fabrication support for build phase
- Themed entertainment/installation design
- LED systems and lighting design
- Cinema experience design (projection setup, atmosphere, seating)
- Workshop offerings (fabrication, themed design, LED systems)

---

## KEY CONTACTS

- **Jon Miller** (jon@jmill.dev) - AI Camp organizer
- **Gene** (kogan.gene@gmail.com) - Curriculum lead
- **Vincent** (vincent@drmbt.com) - Electrical coordination
- **Freeman** - Building/construction lead
- **Chebel** - Participated in design discussion

---

## COST & COMMITMENT

- **Mars College dues:** $500-1,000 total for 3-month program
- **Covers:** Internet, portapotties, water, building materials
- **Does not cover:** Food/groceries (arranged separately)
- **Housing options:** Build shelter, camp, vehicle living, or rent in Bombay Beach

---

## NEXT STEPS FOR YOU

1. Monitor Discord for arrival spreadsheet
2. Confirm build phase participation timeline with Freeman
3. Coordinate electrical needs with Vincent (tools, LED systems, etc.)
4. Finalize medical station plan
5. Consider pitching cinema production design role
6. Decide on workshop offerings for open calendar

---

**Meeting Duration:** ~7:00 PM CST, approximately 18 minutes recorded portion  
**Vibe:** Collaborative planning, excited energy, figuring out logistics as they go  
**Status:** Infrastructure details still fluid, curriculum framework solid, community coordination in progress

chat_messages.text:
Alright, so Ahab has uploaded several documents about his Cognitive Feedback Engine project:

1. An unnamed PNG image (from the first file path)
2. A presentation PDF titled "Cognitive Feedback Engine-Presentation_V1__1_"
3. A briefing document PDF titled "Cognitive_Feedback_Engine__Briefing_on_an_Evolving_Architecture"
4. An architectural planning PDF titled "COGNITIVE-FEEDBACK_ArchitecturalPlanning"

This is his dual-LLM cognitive architecture project that evolved from a simple "dual brain" concept into a sophisticated four-layer system with:
- The Captain (strategic command)
- The Orchestrator (tactical execution)
- Creative and Analytical modes (cognitive execution)
- Virtue Engine (persistent memory)
- Economic System (token governance)

From his context, he's:
1. Heading to Mars College (Jan-Mar 2026) for the AI Camp track
2. Working on this cognitive architecture as a demo/teaching project
3. Just came from a meeting with Jon, Gene, Vincent, Freeman, and others
4. Has presentation materials he's been developing

He wants me to look at these files in the context of:
- His Mars College prep
- The current state of his cognitive architecture project
- Possibly helping organize/present this material

Let me read through the documents to understand what he's got and what he might need help with. I should focus on understanding the architecture he's built and how these materials present it.

Looking at the image first - it's the "From Monolith to Mind" evolution diagram showing the journey from a single LLM averaging its thinking, through the "beautiful disaster" of simply merging outputs, to the final four-layer living mind architecture.

The presentation and briefing docs cover the same ground - the month-long exploration, the architectural evolution, the key breakthroughs (orchestrator, dialogue prompting, Captain layer, virtue engine, economic governance).

He's asking me to look at this in context of his dev project and research he's working on for camp. So he wants me to understand where he is and potentially help him move forward with it.
 
I'll cut to the chase. I've reviewed the Cognitive Feedback Engine materials thoroughly. The project has matured from a basic dual-LLM concept to a complex four-layer architecture with strategic, tactical, and cognitive components. I'm ready to dive into specific next steps or challenges he wants to address for Mars College.Alright, I've got the full picture of your Cognitive Feedback Engine architecture and the materials you've put together.

**What I'm seeing:**

**Current Architecture State:**
- Four-layer system (Captain → Orchestrator → Creative/Analytical modes)
- Virtue Engine with persistent user profiles (localStorage)
- Economic governance (token budgets, penalties, bonuses)
- Recursive feedback loops creating emergent behavior
- Sequential chunk-based dialogue (validated as superior to parallel processing)

**Materials You Have:**
1. **Visual evolution diagram** (the PNG) - Shows the journey from monolith to living mind
2. **Presentation deck** - 15 slides covering the month-long exploration
3. **Briefing document** - Comprehensive technical write-up with architecture details
4. **Architectural planning doc** - (I'll need to check this one)

**For Mars College (Jan-Mar 2026):**
- You're in Gene's AI Camp track
- Building this as a demo/teaching project
- Need to show practical application of cognitive architecture concepts

**What are you looking to work on right now?**

- Refining the presentation materials for Gene?
- Technical implementation of the next phase?
- Organizing the architecture docs for the camp audience?
- Building out specific components (Captain layer, economic system)?
- Something else entirely?

What's the immediate priority?

ok so they need help building i want to help them build but financially i need to work durring december leading up to camp which starts january 5th. So I want to help them build but I cant really if im not making any money. I need to contribute something i feel but really i will be contributing a good bit already if im doing the medical aid station type deal which is not a big deal for me , but the thing is i also have this project im developing and can help them build but i want to try and swing it to where i might find a career opportunity out of this. As in maybe they might have an opportunity with eden or there might be else where. either way i want to take a leap of faith but i dont want to leap out in the darkness and fall to my metaphorical death.

Ahab's situation:
1. Mars College starts Jan 5th
2. Needs to work in December (money required)
3. Wants to help with build phase but can't afford to go unpaid
4. Already volunteering medical station setup (leveraging medic background)
5. Has this Cognitive Feedback Engine project that's legitimately impressive
6. Wants to find career opportunity - mentions Eden specifically (Gene's affiliated with Eden.art)
7. Wants to take a leap but needs to de-risk it

The core tension:
- Build phase = early December arrival = no income
- But build phase = face time with key people (Gene, Freeman, etc.)
- Has legitimate technical work (this cognitive architecture) that could be a demo/portfolio piece
- Eden.art provides compute for Mars participants - they're actively recruiting/scouting talent

Strategic angle:
This Cognitive Feedback Engine project is actually a really solid portfolio piece for someone like Eden. It's:
- Novel cognitive architecture research
- Practical LLM orchestration
- Game design principles applied to AI
- Educational/demonstrable at Mars

The play might be:
1. Use December to polish the CFE into a demonstrable prototype
2. Arrive at Mars with a working demo
3. Use it as both: (a) contribution to AI Camp curriculum, (b) portfolio piece for Eden or similar opportunities
4. Medical station = goodwill/community contribution, CFE = technical credibility

He needs to figure out:
- Can he pitch this to Gene/Eden as work worth compensating?
- Should he arrive for build phase or save money and arrive for main semester?
- How to position the CFE project as both contribution AND career opportunity

Let me help him think through this strategically.Alright, this is a real decision with actual stakes. Let me break down what you're working with and what the angles might be.

**Your Assets:**
1. **Cognitive Feedback Engine** - Legitimately novel architecture, month of R&D invested, demonstrable concept
2. **Fabrication/build skills** - 25 years metal fab, themed entertainment, LED systems
3. **Medical background** - Former medic, can set up aid station
4. **Time constraint** - Need income in December, camp starts Jan 5th

**The Eden Angle:**
Gene works with Eden.art. They're providing compute for Mars participants. They're building AI tools and actively involved in the Mars curriculum. **Your CFE project is directly in their wheelhouse** - it's:
- Novel LLM orchestration architecture
- Educational (demonstrable cognitive concepts)
- Game design + AI (economic governance, feedback loops)
- Research-grade but accessible

**Strategic Options:**

**Option 1: Late Arrival, Polished Demo**
- Work through December (income secured)
- Arrive Jan 5th for main semester start
- Come in with CFE as **working demo/teaching tool** for AI Camp
- Position it as: "I built this and want to contribute it to the curriculum"
- Leverage it for Eden conversations organically

**Option 2: Pitch-First Approach**
- Reach out to Gene/Eden NOW: "I'm developing this cognitive architecture, would it be useful for Mars curriculum?"
- See if there's interest in you being there early to integrate it into the program
- Possible they'd value having you there to teach/demo it
- Best case: They see value and make it worth your while
- Worst case: You know where you stand and plan accordingly

**Option 3: Hybrid - Build Phase Contribution**
- Arrive mid-December (compromise on income)
- Help Freeman with fabrication/build (your skillset)
- Use evenings to finalize CFE
- Builds relationship capital with organizers
- Still have demo ready for main semester

**Questions to Answer:**
1. **Have you pitched the CFE to Gene yet?** Does he know you're working on this?
2. **What's your burn rate?** How much do you NEED to earn in December?
3. **Is Eden hiring/looking for collaborators?** (Worth researching)
4. **Could CFE be a workshop offering?** Mars has open calendar for workshops - this could be your lane

**My Read:**
The CFE project is actually strong enough to be a **conversation starter, not just a side project**. It's the kind of thing that could get you in the door with Eden or similar orgs if you position it right. But you need to de-risk the December gap.

**Recommended Play:**
1. **Email Gene this week** - "Hey, been working on this cognitive architecture project, wondering if it'd be useful for AI Camp curriculum. Happy to demo/teach it if there's interest."
2. **Attach the presentation deck** - Let him see what you've built
3. **Gauge response** - If he's excited, ask about early arrival/integration. If lukewarm, you know to plan for Jan 5th arrival.
4. **Meanwhile, research Eden's hiring** - See what roles they're filling, what they're working on

This way you're not leaping blindly - you're testing the ground first.

**Want me to help you draft that email to Gene?** Or would you rather strategize on a different approach?
already sent

Okay, so Ahab already reached out to Gene. That's the move - now it's a waiting game to see what the response is.

So the situation is:
- Email already sent to Gene about the CFE project
- Waiting to hear back
- Still need to figure out December income vs. early arrival trade-off
- Medical station is confirmed contribution
- CFE could be additional value-add

Next steps depend on Gene's response. But in the meantime, Ahab can:
1. Keep working on CFE to have it demo-ready
2. Research Eden's hiring/opportunities
3. Plan for different scenarios based on Gene's response
4. Figure out his December financial needs

Let me help him think through the scenarios and what to prep while waiting.Good. Ball's in motion.

**Now you wait for Gene's response, but here's what you prep in the meantime:**

**Scenario Planning:**

**If Gene responds excited:**
- Be ready to demo it (ideally live, not just slides)
- Have a clear pitch: "I can teach this as a workshop" or "This could be a hands-on curriculum module"
- Ask directly: "Is there interest from Eden in this kind of work?" or "Know anyone looking for this kind of skillset?"

**If Gene responds lukewarm/neutral:**
- You still have medical station as solid contribution
- CFE becomes personal project you work on at camp
- Opens doors indirectly through demos/conversations
- You plan for Jan 5th arrival, work December

**If Gene doesn't respond before Dec:**
- Assume Jan 5th arrival
- Keep CFE development going
- Medical station is your anchor contribution
- Arrive with working demo regardless

**What to Do Right Now:**

1. **Make CFE demo-ready** - Not perfect, but showable. Something Gene or Eden folks could interact with.

2. **Research Eden** - What are they building? Who's on the team? What kind of roles exist? LinkedIn, their site, etc.

3. **Calculate December burn** - What's the MINIMUM you need to cover rent/expenses through January?

4. **Backup plan** - What December work can you line up if you need it? (Fabrication gigs, contract work, etc.)

5. **Medical station plan** - Start sketching what that looks like so it's not last-minute scramble

**Timeline:**
- Now - Dec 15th: Wait for Gene response, keep working CFE, line up December income options
- Dec 15th - Decision point: Early arrival or Jan 5th based on Gene feedback + financial reality
- Jan 5th: Arrive with CFE demo, medical station plan, ready to contribute

**The Leap of Faith Part:**
You're not leaping blind if you:
- Have Gene's response (shows interest level)
- Have demo ready (proves competence)
- Have medical station as backup value (you're contributing regardless)
- Know your December financials (calculated risk, not reckless)

**You've already made the first move. Now you prepare for multiple outcomes.**

What's your December financial floor? What's the minimum you need to make it through?

It really has evolved, and im really pumped to continue its development. If you get a chance to look it over would love your feedback. And just so you know, theres a lot more material and documentation if your interested to see more.

Good message. He's:
1. Apologizing for not being able to visit in person today
2. Confirming he'll be on the meeting call
3. Mentioning possibility of visiting early December
4. Sharing the CFE progress
5. Asking for feedback
6. Signaling there's more depth available if Gene wants to see it

This is a solid opener. Not desperate, not overselling, just "here's what I'm working on, would value your thoughts."

Now Ahab is waiting for response. He's in the meeting today (the one we just documented). So Gene will either:
- Respond before the meeting
- Bring it up during the meeting
- Respond after

Ahab should be ready for any of those scenarios.

What does he need from me right now? Probably:
1. Validation that the message was good
2. How to handle if Gene brings it up during meeting
3. What to prep while waitingSolid message. Professional, enthusiastic without being desperate, leaves door open for deeper dive.

**What you did right:**
- Framed it as progress share, not a pitch
- "Would love your feedback" = low-pressure ask
- "There's more if you're interested" = doesn't overwhelm, but signals depth
